none: Environmental Impact Statement, Milton-South Disaster Urban Renewal Project, Milton-North Disaster Urban Renewal Project, Turbot Disaster Urban Renewal Project

The Milton-South, Milton-North, and Turbot Disaster Urban Renewal Projects aim to revitalize areas in Milton and Turbot, Pennsylvania, affected by flooding from Tropical Storm Agnes in 1972. The projects involve removing flood-damaged structures, redeveloping land for industrial and commercial use, and improving infrastructure like roads and parks. The goal is to balance the need for economic development with floodplain management, ensuring the community is better protected against future floods while enhancing living conditions and economic opportunities.
Environmental Impact Statement, Milton-South Disaster Urban Renewal Project, Milton-North Disaster Urban Renewal Project, Turbot Disaster Urban Renewal Project

The Milton-South, Milton-North, and Turbot Disaster Urban Renewal Projects aim to revitalize areas in Milton and Turbot, Pennsylvania, affected by flooding from Tropical Storm Agnes in 1972. The projects involve removing flood-damaged structures, redeveloping land for industrial and commercial use, and improving infrastructure like roads and parks. The goal is to balance the need for economic development with floodplain management, ensuring the community is better protected against future floods while enhancing living conditions and economic opportunities.
Borough of Milton and Turbot, Northumberland County, Pennsylvania
Department of Housing and Urban Development
Redevelopment Authority of the County of Northumberland

The Milton-South, Milton-North, and Turbot Disaster Urban Renewal Projects were proposed in response to the significant damage caused by Tropical Storm Agnes in 1972, which resulted in extensive flooding in the area. The projects aimed to revitalize the affected communities by removing damaged structures and redeveloping the land for better economic use while also improving infrastructure. There was some public concern regarding the balance between development and floodplain management, but the initiatives were seen as necessary for the long-term resilience and growth of the communities. Ultimately, these projects were implemented to enhance living conditions and economic opportunities in the region.

"Disaster victims may suffer from disintegration of family relationships, depression, tension, aggravation of previous health problems or appearance of new ones, and inability of school children to adjust to new education environments." — General - Short Term Effects Section

"The projects constitute environmental improvements. In this regard, we do have reservations about the assumption that past estimates of flood plain extent will continue to be accurate in the future." — U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Region III
